title: Advanced Kotlin
description: Generics variance, reified types, value classes, DSLs, contracts, and delegation
lesson:
Senior-Level Kotlin

These features separate senior Kotlin developers from intermediate ones. They appear frequently in library code, custom DSLs, and interview discussions about type safety and performance.

Generics Variance

Variance controls subtype relationships for generic types:

KeywordVarianceMeaningAnalogy
out TCovariantCan only produce T, never consumeList<out Animal> = producer (read-only)
in TContravariantCan only consume T, never produceComparable<in String> = consumer
noneInvariantCan both produce and consumeMutableList<T>
// Declaration-site variance interface Source<out T> { // Producer — T appears only in return positions fun next(): T } interface Sink<in T> { // Consumer — T appears only in parameter positions fun put(item: T) } // Use-site variance (type projection) fun copy(from: Array<out Any>, to: Array<Any>) { for (i in from.indices) to[i] = from[i] } // Star projection — unknown type fun printAll(list: List<*>) { list.forEach { println(it) } // items are Any? }
Rule of thumb: Use out when the class only returns T (producers). Use in when the class only accepts T (consumers). This is the PECS principle: Producer Extends, Consumer Super.

Reified Type Parameters

// Without reified — type erased at runtime fun <T> checkType(obj: Any): Boolean { // return obj is T ← COMPILE ERROR: Cannot check for erased type return false } // With reified — type preserved via inlining inline fun <reified T> checkType(obj: Any): Boolean { return obj is T // ✅ Works! Type is inlined at call site } // Common use: avoiding Class<T> parameter inline fun <reified T : Activity> Context.startActivity() { startActivity(Intent(this, T::class.java)) }

Inline Functions: crossinline & noinline

// crossinline — no non-local returns allowed inline fun runOnUiThread(crossinline action: () -> Unit) { handler.post { action() } // action runs in a different context } // noinline — don't inline this specific lambda inline fun doWork(inlined: () -> Unit, noinline stored: () -> Unit) { inlined() // inlined at call site callbacks += stored // stored as object — can't be inlined }

Value Classes

Zero-overhead type safety wrappers. At runtime, the wrapper is eliminated and only the underlying value exists:

@JvmInline value class UserId(val value: String) @JvmInline value class Email(val value: String) // Compiler prevents mixing up String parameters: fun sendEmail(to: Email, from: UserId) { ... } // sendEmail(userId, email) ← COMPILE ERROR! Type-safe.
When boxing occurs: Value classes get boxed (allocated on heap) when used as nullable types, generic type arguments, or interface implementations. In hot paths, check if boxing happens.

Kotlin Contracts

@OptIn(ExperimentalContracts::class) fun requireNotEmpty(value: String?) { contract { returns() implies (value != null) } if (value.isNullOrEmpty()) throw IllegalArgumentException() } fun process(input: String?) { requireNotEmpty(input) println(input.length) // ✅ Smart cast — compiler knows input is non-null }

DSL Building

// Type-safe builder pattern @DslMarker annotation class HtmlDsl @HtmlDsl class HTML { private val children = mutableListOf<String>() fun body(init: Body.() -> Unit) { val body = Body().apply(init) children += body.render() } fun render() = "<html>${children.joinToString("")}" } @HtmlDsl class Body { private val content = StringBuilder() fun p(text: String) { content.append("<p>$text") } fun render() = "<body>$content" } fun html(init: HTML.() -> Unit): HTML = HTML().apply(init) // Usage: val page = html { body { p("Hello, DSL!") } }
@DslMarker prevents accidental access to outer receivers. Without it, inside body { } you could accidentally call html's methods.

questions.title: Covariance with out
questions.description: Given interface Source<out T> { fun next(): T }, which assignment is valid?
options: val src: Source<Any> = Source<String>() — because String is a subtype of Any and Source is covariant
val src: Source<String> = Source<Any>() — because Any is a supertype
Neither — generic types are always invariant in Kotlin
Both — Kotlin ignores variance annotations
questions.explanation: out T makes Source covariant: Source<String> is a subtype of Source<Any> because String is a subtype of Any. This works because Source only produces T (returns it) — you can safely treat a String producer as an Any producer. The reverse doesn't work: an Any producer might give you something that isn't a String.
hints: out = covariant = subtyping follows the type parameter
A producer of Strings is safely a producer of Any

questions.title: Reified Type Parameter
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to create an extension function that starts an Activity without passing the class explicitly.

blanks.hint: Required for reified — the function body is inlined at the call site
Preserves the type parameter at runtime
Kotlin reflection to get the Java Class

questions.explanation: reified can only be used with inline functions. The compiler inlines the function body at each call site, substituting the actual type. This lets you use T::class at runtime — normally impossible due to type erasure. This pattern eliminates the need for fun launchActivity(clazz: Class<*>).
hints: Type parameters are normally erased at runtime

questions.title: Value Class Boxing
questions.description: Given @JvmInline value class UserId(val value: String), in which case does boxing (heap allocation) occur?
options: val id: UserId = UserId("123") — always boxed
fun process(id: UserId) — boxed because it's a parameter
val id: UserId? = UserId("123") — boxed because nullable types require an object on the heap
Value classes are never boxed under any circumstances
questions.explanation: Value classes are eliminated at runtime (no heap allocation) in most cases — UserId("123") becomes just "123". But boxing occurs in three cases: nullable types (UserId?), generic type arguments (List<UserId>), and when used as an interface type. Nullable requires boxing because the compiler needs to distinguish null from the unwrapped value.
hints: When does the JVM need an actual object rather than the raw underlying value?

questions.title: Contract Smart Cast
questions.description: What does the contract block enable in the calling code?
options: It makes the function throw an exception if the string is null
It tells the compiler that if the function returns true, the receiver is guaranteed non-null — enabling smart cast to String in the if block
It prevents the function from being called on null receivers
It caches the result so repeated calls are faster
questions.explanation: The contract tells the compiler: "if this function returns true, then the receiver (this@isNotNullOrEmpty) is non-null." This enables smart casting in the calling code — inside the if block, the compiler knows name is non-null, so name.length compiles without !! or ?.. This is exactly how isNullOrEmpty() works in the stdlib.
hints: Without the contract, you'd need name!!.length or name?.length inside the if block
The stdlib's isNullOrEmpty() uses this same pattern

questions.title: Type-Safe DSL Builder

checks.message: Use a lambda with receiver for the DSL syntax

questions.explanation: The DSL pattern uses a lambda with receiver: RequestBuilder.() -> Unit. Inside the lambda, this is the RequestBuilder, so you can call url(), method(), header() directly. apply(init) executes the lambda on the builder, then build() creates the immutable result. This is how Kotlin's buildList {}, Ktor's routing, and Compose's Column {} work.
hints: The key is the lambda with receiver: RequestBuilder.() -> Unit
apply() runs the lambda with the builder as 'this'

questions.title: crossinline vs noinline
questions.description: When should you use crossinline on a lambda parameter of an inline function?
options: When you want the lambda to be stored in a variable
When the lambda is invoked from a different execution context (like a Runnable or another lambda) where non-local returns would be unsafe
When you want to allow non-local returns from the lambda
When the lambda is the last parameter and you want trailing lambda syntax
questions.explanation: In an inline function, lambdas can use return to exit the calling function (non-local return). But if the lambda is passed to another execution context (like Runnable { action() }), a non-local return would be unsafe — it can't return from a function that's already exited. crossinline forbids non-local returns while still allowing inlining. noinline is different: it prevents inlining entirely, allowing the lambda to be stored.
hints: Non-local returns are dangerous when the lambda outlives the calling function
Think about what happens if you 'return' inside a Runnable

questions.title: Variance Mismatch
questions.description: This code doesn't compile. Fix the variance to allow adding animals to any compatible list.

checks.message: Fix the variance — think about whether the list is a consumer or producer

questions.explanation: MutableList is invariant: MutableList<Dog> is NOT a subtype of MutableList<Animal>. Why? If it were, you could add a Cat to a list of Dogs — type-unsafe! Using in Animal (contravariance) technically compiles but defeats type safety. The real fix is usually to change the design: use MutableList<Animal> directly, or use List<out Animal> (covariant, read-only) if you only need to read.
hints: MutableList is invariant for good reason — what would happen if you could add a Cat to a Dog list?
Sometimes the fix is in the calling code, not the function signature

questions.title: Property Delegation
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to create a custom property delegate that logs every read and write.

blanks.hint: The interface for delegates of var properties
Called when the property is read
Called when the property is written

questions.explanation: ReadWriteProperty<R, T> is the interface for delegating var properties. getValue is called on read, setValue on write. For val properties, use ReadOnlyProperty (only getValue). The by keyword delegates property access to the delegate object. Common stdlib delegates: lazy, observable, vetoable, and map delegation.
hints: var needs both read and write, val needs only read
The 'by' keyword is syntactic sugar for delegation

questions.title: Destructuring and componentN
options: success: [a, b]
Compile error: must destructure all 3 components
success: null
Compile error: 'items' doesn't match property name 'data'
questions.explanation: Destructuring uses componentN() functions by position, not by name. val (status, items) = result calls result.component1() (status) and result.component2() (data). You don't need to destructure all components — trailing ones can be skipped. The variable name items doesn't need to match the property name data. Caution: reordering data class properties silently changes destructuring!
hints: Destructuring works by position (component1, component2), not by name

questions.title: @DslMarker Purpose
questions.description: What problem does @DslMarker solve in Kotlin DSLs?
options: It makes the DSL functions available only in test code
It prevents accidental access to outer receiver scopes in nested lambdas — without it, inner lambdas could call methods from outer builders
It automatically generates documentation for the DSL
It enables compile-time validation of DSL structure
questions.explanation: Without @DslMarker, in nested builder lambdas, you can accidentally call methods from outer builders. Example: inside body { }, you could call head { } from the outer HTML builder — which is almost certainly a bug. @DslMarker restricts access to only the closest receiver, forcing explicit this@html if you truly need the outer scope. Compose uses this pattern.
hints: Think about what 'this' refers to inside nested lambdas with receivers
